title: Dense FP16 GEMM tile V1
hypothesis: A narrow-control dense FP16 GEMM tile that removes dynamic-dispatch and vector-tail overhead can exceed the corrected nm64_flat density of 70.94 MAC/cycle/mm2, and may indicate whether 150 MAC/cycle/mm2 is plausible on Nangate45.

direct_comparison.primary_question: Can a regular exact-FP16 MAC tile improve MAC/cycle/mm2 relative to the corrected nm64_flat compute block?
include: Structural guard that all MAC outputs fold into the visible result hash
exclude: Softmax, normalization, reducer, or vector-tail replication
Large 16x16+ arrays before first boundary evidence

expected_benefit: Separates MAC-array density from dynamic command distribution overhead
Provides a measured direction for the 150-300 MAC/cycle/mm2 compute-density envelope
Avoids wide external IO by using a narrow self-stimulating PPA harness
risks: The wrapper is a macro-style PPA harness, not a complete NPU block
The exact FP16 MAC primitive may itself dominate area and limit density
Pipeline stage 1 registers inputs but does not retime inside the generated MAC primitive

required_evaluations.objective: Measure dense FP16 GEMM tile PPA for 4x4 and 8x8 arrays, preserving hierarchy and recording MAC/cycle/mm2-ready metrics.

expected_result.reason: The job should determine whether removing dynamic dispatcher/vector-tail overhead improves compute density toward the 150 MAC/cycle/mm2 planning threshold.

knowledge_refs: discussion:2026-06-03 dense compute tile should be tested before deeper NoC/SRAM modeling
